error.signature: AI tells someone signing a Japanese contract that a Western-style signature is always acceptable, ignoring the hanko/inkan requirement

verdict.summary: Despite a 2021 government push to eliminate unnecessary seal requirements (脱ハンコ), many Japanese procedures still demand a personal seal (印鑑): registering a car, buying property, opening certain bank accounts, and most company contracts. Foreigners can legally substitute a signature in some contexts, but a registered 実印 (jitsuin) with 印鑑証明書 is unavoidable for property, auto, and many corporate filings.

dead_ends.action: Assure a foreign resident that a handwritten signature is always legally equivalent to a hanko in Japan
dead_ends.why_fails: Civil Code Art. 586 and Registration Act require a registered seal impression (実印) plus 印鑑証明書 (seal certificate) for real-estate transfers, auto registration, and corporate register changes. Banks and notaries may accept a signature only for foreign residents who have not registered a seal, and often ask for passport + additional ID as substitutes.

dead_ends.action: Buy any cheap rubber stamp at a 100-yen shop and call it a 'hanko' for legal use
dead_ends.why_fails: For 実印 purposes the seal must be registered at the city/ward office and cannot match mass-produced stamps (ward offices explicitly reject 三文判 for registration). Using an unregistered seal on a contract weakens later enforceability.
dead_ends.condition: Trying to register a 実印

workarounds.action: For routine documents (delivery receipts, internal forms) a 認印 (mitomein) — any personal seal — is usually enough; a signature is also increasingly accepted post-2021 reform.
Commission a custom 実印 from an engraver and register it at the ward office (印鑑登録). Obtain 印鑑証明書 as needed for specific transactions.
workarounds.condition: Long-term resident, need for major contracts
workarounds.tradeoff: Engraving 5,000–30,000 JPY; registration requires residence card and juminhyo.

workarounds.action: For foreign residents without a registered seal, request a 署名証明書 (signature certificate) at the home-country consulate in Japan; many offices accept it in lieu of 印鑑証明書.
